analogy: Think of "States of Matter" through the chapter's own opening images: Thermodynamics It is the only physical theory of universal content concerning which I am convinced that, within the framework of the applicability of its basic concepts, it will never be overthrown. Albert Einstein Chemical energy stored by molecules can be released as heat during chemical... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: THERMODYNAMICS 137, 5.1 Thermodynamic Terms, 5.1.1 The system and the surroundings, 5.1.2 Types of the system. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
States of Matter is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that thermodynamics It is the only physical theory of universal content concerning which I am convinced that, within the framework of the applicability of its basic concepts, it will never be overthrown.

The text explains that albert Einstein Chemical energy stored by molecules can be released as heat during chemical reactions when a fuel like methane, cooking gas or coal burns in air.

The text explains that the chemical energy may also be used to do mechanical work when a fuel burns in an engine or to provide electrical energy through a galvanic cell like dry cell.

The text explains that thus, various forms of energy are interrelated and under certain conditions, these may be transformed from one form into another.

The text explains that the study of these energy transformations forms the subject matter of thermodynamics.

The text explains that the laws of thermodynamics deal with energy changes of macroscopic systems involving a large number of molecules rather than microscopic systems containing a few molecules.

The text explains that thermodynamics is not concerned about how and at what rate these energy transformations are carried out, but is based on initial and final states of a system undergoing the change.

The text explains that laws of thermodynamics apply only when a system is in equilibrium or moves from one equilibrium state to another equilibrium state.

The text explains that macroscopic properties like pressure and temperature do not change with time for a system in equilibrium state.

The text explains that in this unit, we would like to answer some of the important questions through thermodynamics, like: How do we determine the energy changes involved in a chemical reaction/process?

The text explains that to what extent do the chemical reactions proceed?

The text explains that uniT 5 After studying this Unit, you will be able to • explain the terms : system and surroundings; • discriminate between close, open and isolated systems; • explain internal energy, work and heat; • state first law of thermodynamics and express it mathematically; • calculate energy changes as work and heat contributions in chemical systems; • explain state functions: U, H. • correlate ∆U and ∆H; • measure experimentally ∆U and ∆H; • define standard states for ∆H; • calculate enthalpy changes for various types of reactions; • state and apply Hess’s law of constant heat summation; • differentiate between extensive and intensive properties; • define spontaneous and non- spontaneous processes; • e x p l a i n e n t r o p y a s a thermodynamic state function and apply it for spontaneity; • explain Gibbs energy change (∆G); and • establish relationship between ∆G and spontaneity, ∆G and equilibrium constant.

The text explains that tHERMODYNAMICS 137 5.1 Thermodynamic Terms We are interested in chemical reactions and the energy changes accompanying them.

The text explains that for this we need to know certain thermodynamic terms.

The text explains that 5.1.1 The system and the surroundings A system in thermodynamics refers to that part of universe in which observations are made and remaining universe constitutes the surroundings.

The text explains that the surroundings include everything other than the system.

The text explains that system and the surroundings together constitute the universe.

The text explains that the universe = The system + The surroundings However, the entire universe other than the system is not affected by the changes taking place in the system.

The text explains that therefore, for all practical purposes, the surroundings are that portion of the remaining universe which can interact with the system.

The text explains that usually, the region of space in the neighbourhood of the system constitutes its surroundings.

The text explains that for example, if we are studying the reaction between two substances A and B kept in a beaker, the beaker containing the reaction mixture is the system and the room where the beaker is kept is the surroundings (Fig.

The text explains that 5.1 System and the surroundings the system from the surroundings is called boundary.

The text explains that this is designed to allow us to control and keep track of all movements of matter and energy in or out of the system.

The text explains that 5.1.2 Types of the system We, further classify the systems according to the movements of matter and energy in or out of the system.

The text explains that open System In an open system, there is exchange of energy and matter between system and surroundings [Fig.

The text explains that the presence of reactants in an open beaker is an example of an open system*.

The text explains that closed System In a closed system, there is no exchange of matter, but exchange of energy is possible between system and the surroundings [Fig.

The text explains that the presence of reactants in a closed vessel made of conducting material e.g., copper or steel is an example of a closed system.

The text explains that note that the system may be defined by physical boundaries, like beaker or test tube, or the system may simply be defined by a set of Cartesian coordinates specifying a particular volume in space.

The text explains that it is necessary to think of the system as separated from the surroundings by some sort of wall which may be real or imaginary.

The text explains that the wall that separates * We could have chosen only the reactants as system then walls of the beakers will act as boundary.

The text explains that isolated System In an isolated system, there is no exchange of energy or matter between the system and the surroundings [Fig.

The text explains that the presence of reactants in a thermos flask or any other closed insulated vessel is an example of an isolated system.

The text explains that 5.1.3 The state of the system The system must be described in order to make any useful calculations by specifying quantitatively each of the properties such as its pressure (p), volume (V), and temperature (T ) as well as the composition of the system.

The text explains that we need to describe the system by specifying it before and after the change.

The text explains that you would recall from your Physics course that the state of a system in mechanics is completely specified at a given instant of time, by the position and velocity of each mass point of the system.

The text explains that in thermodynamics, a different and much simpler concept of the state of a system is introduced.

The text explains that it does not need detailed knowledge of motion of each particle because, we deal with average measurable properties of the system.

The text explains that we specify the state of the system by state functions or state variables.

The text explains that the state of a thermodynamic system is described by its measurable or macroscopic (bulk) properties.

The text explains that we can describe the state of a gas by quoting its pressure (p), volume (V), temperature (T ), amount (n) etc.

The text explains that variables like p, V, T are called state variables or state functions because their values depend only on the state of the system and not on how it is reached.

The text explains that in order to completely define the state of a system it is not necessary to define all the properties of the system; as only a certain number of properties can be varied independently.

The text explains that this number depends on the nature of the system.

The text explains that 5.12 Enthalpies of formation of CO(g), CO2 (g), N2 O(g) and N2 O4 (g) are –110, – 393, 81 and 9.7 kJ mol–1 respectively.

The text explains that find the value of ∆r H for the reaction: N 2 O4 (g) + 3CO(g) → N2 O(g) + 3CO2 (g) 5.13 Given N 2 (g) + 3H2 (g) → 2NH3 (g); ∆r H = –92.4 kJ mol–1 What is the standard enthalpy of formation of NH3 gas?

The text explains that 5.14 Calculate the standard enthalpy of formation of CH 3 OH(l) from the following data: CH3 OH (l) + 3 2 O2 (g) → CO2 (g) + 2H2 O(l) ; ∆r H = –726 kJ mol–1 C(graphite) + O2 (g) → CO2 (g) ; ∆c H = –393 kJ mol–1 H 2 (g) + 1 2 O2 (g) → H2 O(l); ∆f H = –286 kJ mol–1 .

The text explains that 5.15 Calculate the enthalpy change for the process CCl4 (g) → C(g) + 4 Cl(g) and calculate bond enthalpy of C – Cl in CCl4 (g). ∆vap H(CCl4 ) = 30.5 kJ mol–1 . ∆f H (CCl4 ) = –135.5 kJ mol–1 . ∆a H (C) = 715.0 kJ mol–1 , where ∆a H is enthalpy of atomisation ∆a H (Cl2 ) = 242 kJ mol–1 5.16 For an isolated system, ∆U = 0, what will be ∆S ?

The text explains that 5.17 For the reaction at 298 K, 2A + B → C ∆H = 400 kJ mol –1 and ∆S = 0.2 kJ K–1 mol–1 At what temperature will the reaction become spontaneous considering ∆H and ∆S to be constant over the temperature range.

The text explains that 5.18 For the reaction, 2 Cl(g) → Cl2 (g), what are the signs of ∆H and ∆S ?

The text explains that 5.19 For the reaction 2 A(g) + B(g) → 2D(g) ∆U  = –10.5 kJ and ∆S = –44.1 JK –1 .

The text explains that calculate ∆G for the reaction, and predict whether the reaction may occur spontaneously.

Source: NCERT Class 11 Chemistry, Chapter 5: States of Matter
